coredata.dc:title: Simple-to-use and portable device for free chlorine determination based on microwave-assisted synthesized carbon dots and smartphone images
coredata.dc:creator: Uriarte, D.
Vidal, E.
Canals, A.
Domini, C.E.
Garrido, M.
coredata.dc:description: A new portable and simple 3D printed device was designed for free chlorine determination in water samples. The analytical method was based on the quenching caused by free chlorine on the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots (CD) synthesized from citric acid and urea. The fluorescence was captured through the camera of a smartphone, which was coupled to the 3D printed device, and the images were processed using the RGB system by the ImageJ 1.51q software. The proposed method was selective and precise (RSD% 4.6, for n = 6), and the trueness of the results was evaluated by comparing the results obtained with those recovered by the spectrophotometric method 4500-Cl G (standard method), with good agreement between them. Moreover, the remarkable correlation between the CD signal and the free chlorine concentration resulted in a determination with low detection limits (limit of detection of 6 μg L−1 and limit of quantification of 20 μg L−1). Therefore, the new method and the related portable device could be considered a fast, economical and reliable alternative for the on-site determination of free chlorine in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portable device
Smartphone
Carbon dots
Fluorimetric determination
Free chlorine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Digital image acquisition and region selection
Interaction between free chlorine and carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Agencia Nacional de Promoción Científica y Tecnológica

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank the financial support received from Universidad Nacional del Sur (PGI 24/Q099 and 24/Q086) and the ANPCyT (PICT-201-0659). The authors also thank Consejo Nacional de Investigaciones Científicas y Técnicas (CONICET) particularly for the doctoral grant of D. Uriarte.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme showing the different components of the portable device.
TEM image of the carbon dots (100000x). Inset: Histogram corresponding to the diameter distribution.
Contour plot of 10 mg L−1 carbon dots in the absence (A) and presence (B) of 1.0 mg L−1 free chlorine.
Effect of different interfering anions and cations on the carbon dots signal in the presence (black) or absence (white) of 0.3 mg L−1 free chlorine.

Elliptical confidence region for the comparison between the proposed method and the 4500-Cl G spectrophotometric method, at a significance level of 5%. The black point is the centre of the ellipse (0.007; 0.96) and the red circle is the theoretical point (0.00; 1.00). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Recovery values for the determination of free chlorine using both the reference and the proposed method.

row.entry: Reference Method Free chloride found (mg L−1)
Proposed Method Free chloride found (mg L−1)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the analytical performance of different methods for free chlorine determination.

row.entry: Orthotolidine as a reagent. Detection based on μPAD and scanner. Image analysis in the RGB system.
Tap, spring, river, and well water
DPD as a reagent. Detection using a smartphone based device. Quantification through images analysis.
DPD as a reagent. Detection using a smartphone based device. Quantification through illumination analysis.
Tap, drinking, swimming tank, and water purifiers
Carbon dots synthesized from tea residues (with sugar and milk) as a sensor
Tap, drinking, boiling, river, and lake water
DPD as a reagent. Fiber optic sensor coupled to a sequential injection-Lab on valve. 10 min of incubation time.
Ascorbic acid reacts with free chlorine (20 min) The remaining ascorbic acid decolored MnO2 nanosheets suspension (15 min).
Carbon dots based on citric acid, l-tartaric acid and ethanediamine as a sensor
Quantum dots of ZnO modified with (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) as a sensor. Stable signals after 60 s
Metal-MOFs nanoplates based on Aluminium (III) chloride hexahydrate and 2-amino-1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (NH2–H2BDC)
Ratiometric fluorescence probe using phenylendiamine based carbon dots and [Ru(bpy)3]2+
Carbon nitride nanoparticles based on tartaric acid and urea as a sensor
Tap Water, Boiling Tap Water, and Swimming Water Samples
Carbon nitride based on guanidine hydrochloride and EDTA as a sensor
Tap, drinking, and swimming- pool water
Carbon dots based on citric acid and urea. Detection using a smartphone based device. Quantification through images analysis in the RGB system.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The concentrations are expressed in the same units used in the cited publication.

head.ce:title: Simple-to-use and portable device for free chlorine determination based on microwave-assisted synthesized carbon dots and smartphone images

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: INQUISUR, Departamento de Química, Universidad Nacional Del Sur (UNS)-CONICET, Av. Alem 1253, 8000, Bahía Blanca, Argentina
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: INQUISUR, Departamento de Quimica, Universidad Nacional del Sur (UNS)-CONICET, Av. Alem 1253, 8000 Bahia Blanca, Argentina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Analítica, Nutrición y Bromatología, Instituto Universitario de Materiales, Universidad de Alicante, Apdo. 99, 03080, Alicante, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nutrición y Bromatología, Instituto Universitario de Materiales

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Quimica Analitica, Nutricion y Bromatologia, Instituto Universitario de Materiales, Universidad de Alicante, Apdo. 99, 03080 Alicante, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new portable and simple 3D printed device was designed for free chlorine determination in water samples. The analytical method was based on the quenching caused by free chlorine on the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots (CD) synthesized from citric acid and urea. The fluorescence was captured through the camera of a smartphone, which was coupled to the 3D printed device, and the images were processed using the RGB system by the ImageJ 1.51q software. The proposed method was selective and precise (RSD% 4.6, for n = 6), and the trueness of the results was evaluated by comparing the results obtained with those recovered by the spectrophotometric method 4500-Cl G (standard method), with good agreement between them. Moreover, the remarkable correlation between the CD signal and the free chlorine concentration resulted in a determination with low detection limits (limit of detection of 6 μg L−1 and limit of quantification of 20 μg L−1). Therefore, the new method and the related portable device could be considered a fast, economical and reliable alternative for the on-site determination of free chlorine in water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Water quality is critical to safeguard human health and safety. In 2010, the United Nations (UN) General Assembly declared that safe and clean drinking-water, and sanitation are human rights. Accordingly, the World Health Organization (WHO) published the Guidelines for drinking-water quality, whose main goal is to protect public health associated with drinking-water quality [1]. Over the years, several strategies have been adopted to improve the quality of drinking water. Among them, chlorination is one of the most widely used disinfection processes because it is simple and inexpensive [1]. However, chlorine must be used with care, as it can react with naturally occurring organic species to produce different disinfection byproducts (DBPs), with trihalomethanes (THMs) and haloacetic acids (HAAs) being the main DBPs, which are considered as carcinogenic [2,3]. According to the WHO, control of DBPs could be achieved through monitoring precursor compounds, for which the determination of chlorine residues in drinking water is of real importance [1]. The free residual chlorine is defined as the sum of dissolved Cl2, HClO and ClO−, and it is normally expressed as mass of Cl2 by liter (mg L−1 or ppm) [4].

The standard methods normally used for the determination of free residual chlorine are the titrimetric procedure, which uses FAS (ferrous ammonium sulphate) and DPD (N,N-diethyl-1,4-phenylenediamine) [5], and the colorimetric method 4500-Cl G, which also uses DPD [5,6]. Neither method is portable. However, sometimes the free chlorine concentration, as well as other parameters such as pH or turbidity, must be measured on site [1] In this regard, the development of improved analytical methods for the in-situ monitoring of free chlorine is a topic of interest in the literature, and new analytical methods based on simple and low-cost test equipment are needed.

Several analytical methods based on portable devices have been developed in the last years for chlorine determination. For instance, Jović et al. proposed inkjet printed silver electrodes for free chlorine analysis in water that could be adapted for on-site analyses, although with a relatively high detection limit [7]. Also, Xiong et al. developed a sophisticated device based on a solution-gated graphene transistor, which was used for continuous and real time determination of free chlorine in tap water with low detection limits [8]. Other portable devices are based on optical techniques. One of them consists of a polymeric film supported on a waveguide that makes it possible to interferometrically monitor the changes in the refractive index of the polymer after a reaction with free chlorine [9]. However, the concentrations were approximately 60% of the ones obtained with the DPD method. Another method based on evanescent-wave phenomenon was reported by Xiong et al. [10], which uses a microfluidic arrangement that acts both as a flow and detection cell. This fast colorimetric determination, based on DPD reagents, reaches a low detection limit. On the other hand, a microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD) has been proposed for detection of free chlorine [11]. In this method, the orthotolidine is immobilized on the paper and the images taken are processed using the RGB system. Several studies using different portable devices with webcams and smartphones as detection systems have been recently published [12–15]. Danchana et al. proposed a method for the colorimetric determination of hypochlorite using a new device created by 3D printing for coupling a webcam as a detection system. In this sense, 3D printers are versatile tools increasingly used as a complement in the design and implementation of different analytical methods [12]. Among the methods using smartphones for free chlorine determination, there are two based on DPD colorimetric reaction [13,14], whereas the other uses 3,3′-5,5′ tetramethylbenzidine and methionine stabilized gold nanoclusters, and the signal is measured by a ratiometric fluorescence method [15]. Regarding the use of smartphones, they are a promising tool for the development of analytical methods since they have the ability to acquire and store an enormous amount of data, such as images, using a wireless connection [16,17]. Using smartphones for fluorescent determinations has been previously reported using different fluorophores, including carbon dots [18–20], with further data analysis using the RGB system. However, to the best of our knowledge, no analytical method using smartphones was proposed for free chlorine determination based on carbon dots fluorescence changes.

In this study, a new 3D printed portable device compatible with a smartphone was designed as a platform for a new analytical method devoted to free chlorine determination in water samples. The new methodology is based on measuring the quenching effect of free chlorine species on the fluorescence of carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and urea. The goal of our research was to develop a device that is user-friendly but also provides reliable results in the analysis of water samples. The performance of the proposed analytical method was satisfactory regarding trueness, precision, linear range, limit of detection and selectivity. The method was successfully applied to free chlorine determination in swimming-pool and tap water samples and the results were in good agreement with the ones obtained with the 4500-Cl G spectrophotometric method [5,6]. Thus, the method and the associated portable device could be recommended as a rapid, inexpensive and reliable choice for its use in the on-site measurements of free chlorine, even for inexperienced users.

Urea (≥99.5% purity) was purchased from Anedra (Buenos Aires, Argentina). Citric acid (≥99.5% purity) was supplied by Cicarelli (Santa Fe, Argentina). All standard solutions were prepared in ultrapure water (Millipore, Bedford, USA). A sodium hypochlorite stock standard solution was prepared –standardized by iodometric titration– and storage in air-tight bottles below 20 °C protected from light. Working standard solutions were freshly prepared in ultrapure water from appropriate dilution of the stock standard solution. Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions, ranging from pH 3.0 to 9.0, were used for pH related experiments.

The morphology of the synthesized carbon dots was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JSM 100 CXII, Tokio, Japan) operating at 100 kV. The carbon dots solution was dropped onto a copper grid and then evaporated in order to obtain the TEM images. KBr pellets with the lyophilized carbon dots (1% w/w) were prepared to record the infrared spectra (Fourier Transform IR spectrometer, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific). UV–Vis spectra were measured in an Agilent 8453 spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence ones were recorded using a JASCO FP-6500 spectrofluorometer.

The portable device was designed in such a way that a smartphone could be coupled to a dark chamber that makes it possible to measure the fluorescence emission under controlled lighting conditions. The chamber in turn was connected to a cell holder in which the sample is placed in a plastic cuvette (~5 mL in volume), as shown in Fig. 1 . In addition, the cell holder incorporated a light-emitting diode (LED) with an emission wavelength about 400 nm, which was located at 90° from the detector, i.e. the smartphone camera (Fig. 1). The dimensions and details of the 3D printed device could be found elsewhere [21]. Moreover, it is worth mentioning that the device requires neither lens, mirror reflectors, plastic diffusers nor other optical components.

The carbon dots were prepared by pyrolysis using a microwave oven, as reported in the literature [22], with some modifications. 10 mL of an aqueous solution (1.7 mol L−1 in urea and 0.5 mol L−1 in citric acid) were heated in a home microwave oven at 700 W for 2.5 min in order to obtain a black solid. After that, ultrapure water (15.0 mL) was added and stirred for 30 min. The dispersion was centrifuged (1200 G, 4 min) and the supernatant placed in a dialysis bag (Spectra/Por® Dialysis membrane, Biotech CE Tubing, cut off: 100–500 Da) inside a container with ultrapure water for 3 days. The water was changed every 24 h.

A set of calibration standards was prepared as follows: the appropriate amounts of sodium hypochlorite working solution were placed in a 5.00 mL volumetric flask adding 500 μL of BR buffer (pH 4.0) and 200 μL of purified carbon dots solution, finally filling up to the mark with ultrapure water. Calibration standards were prepared in triplicate. The %RG vs. the chlorine concentration was linear in the range of 20–500 μg L−1 of free chlorine.

Samples of tap, purified drinking water and swimming pool water were used without any treatment and analysed in triplicate. All the samples were also analysed by the 4500-Cl G spectrophotometric method [5,6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digital image acquisition and region selection
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence response of carbon dots in the presence of free chlorine was weighted using a normalized mean of both R and G channels, in accordance with the following equation:
Eq. 1
% R G = 100
×
0.5
R
R o
+
0.5
G
G o
where R and G are the values obtained for the red and green channels, respectively, with a solution containing free chlorine, whereas Ro and Go are the ones corresponding to the blank. The recorded %RG values were related to the concentration of free chlorine, which acts as a quencher, by the Stern-Volmer equation, (%RG0/%RG = KSV [free chlorine] + 1, where %RG0 corresponds to the emission intensity in absence of quencher, %RG is the signal in the presence of quencher and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant) [23]. The images were taken with a smartphone (Samsung Galaxy J1 Ace) using a free software application (HedgeCam2) to set a spatial resolution of 1600 × 1200 pixels.

Image fluorescence analysis was performed on all smartphone images using freely available software (ImageJ 1.51q software). The chosen region of interest (ROI) was a single square selection (2400 pixels), selected in the zone of maximum intensity. The extension and position of this area was set for all the samples, and the distribution of RGB values of every pixel was contained in histograms. Then, the image processing was automatically repeated in the same way for every image. The mean value of the channels was used as an analytical signal.

The selectivity of the proposed method was studied by testing the possible effects of some anions and cations eventually present in the analysed water samples in the fluorescence response of the carbon dots. Thus, cations such as sodium, potassium and calcium (up to 100 mg L−1), zinc, copper(II) and zirconium (up to 5 mg L−1), chromium(VI), lead(IV), aluminium and iron(III) (up to 1 mg L−1), mercury (I and II), cadmium and arsenic(V) (up to 0.1 mg L−1) were tested. Also, fluoride (up to 10 mg L−1), sulphate (100 mg L−1) and chloride (100 mg L−1) were investigated. The above-mentioned ions were added to standard solutions prepared as in section 2.5, with and without 0.3 mg L−1 of free chlorine. Then, the fluorescence was measured.

Different carbon dots arise from diverse raw materials [24]. The composition and structure of these new materials are normally studied by a series of characterization techniques with different results. Sometimes these techniques do not give conclusive insights and there is not a generalized agreement regarding the actual composition and the relationship between the structure and the properties of the carbon dots [24–27]. Recent studies have suggested that some molecular fluorophores, generated during bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots, could be mainly responsible for the luminescence features of these nanoparticles [28]. Particularly, in carbon dots derived from the carbonization of citric acid, several fluorescent molecules have been identified along with carbon cores, depending on the used doping agent. Accordingly, molecules such as imidazo [1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo- (IPCA), 4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo [3,4-c]pyridine 1,3,6(2H,5H)-trione (HPPT), citrazinic acid and its derivatives were reported [22,25,27–29]. Indeed, some studies have demonstrated that the properties of these fluorescent molecules resemble the ones of carbon dots regarding both their absorption and luminescent features [30,31]. On the other hand, some authors have studied the intrinsic composition of carbon dots based on citric acid [25,29–31]. They have proposed a structure based on agglomerates of concentric nanostructures originated during the synthesis at high temperatures, composed by multiple layers of molecular organic precursors, forming an arrangement similar to graphene oxides [32]. The presence of functional groups in the structure, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl or heteroatoms, like N, contribute to the electronic state heterogeneity. In addition, the possibility of self-assembly of these nanoparticles in H-type aggregates also contributes to generate new different and multiple excitation states that give rise to the redshift usually observed in the carbon dots [33,34]. In summary, the typical heterogeneity of carbon dots, which is reflected in their photoluminescent behaviour, could be attributed not only to the conformation of these carbonaceous nanoparticles with low quantum yields, but also to highly fluorescent molecules generated during the bottom-up synthesis [27,32]. Furthermore, other authors that have studied the synthesis of the same type of carbon dots argue for the formation of a hybrid material when these fluorophores are in the same solution together with the carbon cores [22,34]. Besides, its properties and reactivity are different than the ones of the individual entities [22,30,31].

On the other hand, several recent publications remark the importance of an appropriate purification of bottom-up synthesized citric acid/urea carbon dots, since they have demonstrated that the different fractions of dialysis (i.e. retentate and dialysate) yielded different responses towards some analytes [35]. The authors considered that an exhaustive separation is crucial to assign correctly the quenching effect to the carbon cores, the oligomers, or the molecular fluorophores generated during the bottom-up synthesis. Despite the importance of properly assigning the luminescence properties to the carbon cores or molecules, the hybrid materials per se show distinguishable properties that can be used to develop new applications based on the as-synthesized citric acid/urea carbon dots [22].

In this context, we selected a well-studied type of carbon dots based on the bottom-up pyrolytic process of citric acid and urea, aided by microwave irradiation [22]. The purification process performed using low molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) dialysis bags guarantees the removal of the smaller raw materials, whereas the carbon dots, and also some fluorescent molecules are retained, such as citrazinic acid and its derivatives, among others [35].

Even though these carbon dots studied, some analyses were carried out in order to corroborate the similarity with the previously reported ones. In this sense, TEM (Fig. 2 ) was used to investigate the nanoparticles size and morphology. As it can be seen, the dried product is presented as well-dispersed spherical shape agglomerates with a size between 4 and 10 nm. No polymer-like shapes were observed but some agglomerates were present. The inset in Fig. 2 shows the histogram, based on the count of nearly 1000 particles, which have an average diameter of 6.6 nm.

On the other hand, the IR spectrum (Fig. S1) shows the typical broad absorption band above 3000-3500 cm−1 that corresponds to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations [36]. The sharp band around 1700 cm−1 could be ascribed to unsaturated CO groups [37], while the shoulder about 1620 cm−1 was assigned to stretching of CC [38]. In addition, bands in the region between 1100 and 1400 cm−1 are assigned to vibration of C–O (stretching), C–N (stretching) and –NH2 (bending) [36]. These bands are in accordance with ones reported in the literature for similar hybrid material [22], and could be related to the presence of an unsaturated carbon structure with functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl. Moreover, these absorption bands indicate the presence of nitrogen in the composition of the nanoparticles or fluorescence oligomers.

The UV–Vis spectrum of the carbon dots (Fig. S2) shows the typical absorptions bands at around 245 nm and 340 nm, which are related to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. The π-π* transition could be assigned to conjugated carbon rings of either molecular fluorophores or the ones within the core of carbon dots [39–41]. On the other hand, the n-π* transitions are associated with doping and surface functionalization of carbon dots, mainly with the carbonyl bond [30,40–42]. Moreover, another band appears at long wavelengths, at about 410 nm, with a similar intensity to the 340 nm band. This absorption could be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the pyridone ring [29]. All these bands are the same as those reported in the literature for the same type of carbon dots [22,29].

The contour of the excitation/emission fluorescence spectra recorded for the carbon dots is shown in Fig. 3 . The photoluminescence profile exhibited excitation/emission maxima at 360/445 and 425/521 nm (Fig. 3A). In addition, the system also showed excitation-dependent emission, with the typical redshift observed in most of the carbon dots (Fig. S3 A). This behaviour has already been described by other authors and explained in terms of the ground state heterogeneity originated in H-type agglomerates, which would explain the large Stokes shift observed in the hybrid system (∽ 100 nm) and the fluorescence origin [33,34]. In accordance with previous reports, the emission band around 520 nm may arise from lower-weight fluorescent molecules [22]. Nevertheless, Sharma et al. proposed that the emission at about 460 nm is due to the carbon dots as individual units, and the emission at 520 nm could be ascribed to the H-type aggregates [33]. Some experiments were performed in order to verify the presence of these H-type aggregates. In this sense, Fig. S3 B shows the fluorescence emission spectra recorded at different temperatures (25 and 75 °C). It is expected that as the temperature increases, the population of the excitonic states of the H-type aggregates will increase, with a corresponding increase in fluorescence emission [33]. As can be seen in Fig. S3 B, the fluorescence emission (excitation wavelength 425 nm) was higher at high temperature, confirming the presence of this type of aggregates [33]. The opposite behaviour was observed for the fluorescence emission spectrum when excited at 360 nm which was attributed to the molecular fluorophores. In this case, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing temperature, as described by Sharma et al. [33]. In our case, the dialysis process with a small MWCO (<1 KDa), made it possible to purify the system retaining some molecular fluorophores that interact with the carbon cores, enriching the photoluminescence properties of the system [22,35]. The observed optical features are in accordance with the ones previously reported [22,29,35]. The purified carbon dots have a quantum yield of about 18.1%, which was calculated following the procedure suggested by William et al. [43], using quinine sulphate in 0.10 mol L−1 H2SO4 (Φ = 0.54) as a reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between free chlorine and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, the fluorescence of the carbon dots based on citric acid and urea is sensibly quenched by the presence of free chlorine. Several authors have also reported this decrease in the fluorescent intensity of carbon dots [3,44]. The effect of free chlorine on the hybrid system can be observed in Fig. 3B. In a general way, free chlorine affects the whole spectrum. Interestingly, the most affected band is the one located at an excitation/emission of 425/521 nm, which could be ascribed to some fluorophores and/or H-type agglomerates [22,33]. In addition, free chlorine also caused a shift in the emission band located at about 360/445 nm, which was moved toward 345/430 nm (Fig. 3B). This change in excitation/emission reveals that the oxidation ability of free chlorine not only allows the fluorescence to be extinguished by decreasing its intensity, but also would modify the emission states of the carbon dots.

The changes at ground state levels after the addition of free chlorine were analysed in the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S2). It was found that the absorption shoulders at higher energies disappeared while the intense absorption bands considerably decreased and redshifted. In this regard, Spano [45] describes that the formation of H-type aggregates typically causes a blue shift in absorption spectra. Therefore, a red shift is expected to occur when these aggregates disappear due to the effect of the free chlorine.

Other authors also report that the free chlorine species are able to strongly affect the CDs surface by oxidation of the functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen [36]. The oxidation of the CDs surface produces defects which could be responsible for the observed red shift in the fluorescence spectra [46]. The more oxidized the CDs surface, the greater the redshift is. Fig. S3C shows the effect of the addition of free chlorine on the fluorescence excitation spectra, where the redshift is more pronounced as the concentration of free chlorine increases.

Therefore, the oxidation ability of the free chlorine could affect the system in two ways. One of them arises from the surface oxidation of the carbon dots, as some authors have previously suggested [36,46] and the other one could be related to the influence of the free chlorine on the agglomerates of the hybrid system.

Based on the fluorescence of the carbon dots hybrid system and the quenching effect produced by free chlorine, a new analytical method was proposed for its determination in water samples. So as to develop a portable system that can be handled by non-expert users, a simple and user-friendly device was designed. Commercial assay kits designed for free chlorine determinations in water are semi-quantitative, especially for swimming-pool and tap water. Normally, visual inspection depends largely on the user's subjective perception of colours, which can bias the results. Moreover, the difficulty of recognizing small changes in colour intensity limits the precision of these methodologies [47]. Accordingly, digital image analysis appears to be a simple and inexpensive approach to overcome these drawbacks while maintaining portability [48].
The operational conditions were optimized in accordance with the %RG, in order to obtain the maximum sensitivity in the measurements.

The optimal dimensions of the device were studied by printing devices of different sizes. The analytical signal was higher when the light source was closer to the sample cell. However, the background noise also increased as the LED was closer to the sample, mainly due to the LED light dispersion. Accordingly, a cylinder of 5 mm in length and 2 mm i.d. was printed and placed between the LED and the cell holder in order to minimize the light scattering phenomenon and improve the uniformity of the recorded image. As already commented in section 2.6, it is important to guarantee a fixed region of interest (ROI) to avoid spatial variations of the signal, which could arise due to differences in intensity in the various regions of the image produced by the scattering of the emitted light. For this purpose, the acquired images were analysed using the ImageJ software. The program was set to import images, crop them to 150 × 70 pixels ―all of them in the fixed ROI― and examine their red-green-blue contributions to the RGB system.

The carbon dots in water exhibited a bright green colour under UV light. Emission-related information could be collected in RGB channels and used to detect free chlorine. In order to select the best analytical signal, solutions of carbon dots with different amounts of free chlorine were prepared and their images were recorded in the portable device to analyse the eventual correlation between the concentration and the response in the RGB system. The blue channel (B) poorly correlated with the concentration of free chlorine and also exhibited high background noise. Hence, the B channel was omitted in the subsequent studies. On the other hand, both R and G channels correlated equally well with the chlorine concentration, so we chose a combination of these two channels as an analytical signal for further studies (%RG, see Eq. (1)).

The photostability of the carbon dots for the proposed method was tested by irradiating a solution of carbon dots with the LED source for 2.5 h (excitation wavelength about 400 nm and power ca. 0.120 W). Although the power of the light source was considerably lower than that of conventional lamps (90 W), it was sufficient to test the stability of measurements throughout the time for the purposes of this study (Fig. S4A). On the other hand, the ionic strength effect (Fig. S4B) was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence of a carbon dots solution after the addition of different amounts of NaCl. There were no appreciable effects on the signal up to a concentration of 1.0 mol L−1 NaCl.

Also, the effect of the concentration of carbon dots was investigated by preparing three carbon dots solutions with different concentration levels (5, 10 and 17 mg L−1), to which different concentrations of free chlorine were added (Fig. S5). As the carbon dots concentration diminished, the sensitivity towards chlorine concentration increased. However, further dilutions of the carbon dots concentration gave much lower signals. Therefore, the optimal concentration of carbon dots was set in 5 mg L−1.

The influence of the pH was also tested in both the carbon dots and the mixture of carbon dots and free chlorine. The pH was varied within the range of 3.0–9.0, as it can be seen in Fig. S6. The fluorescence signal of the nanoparticles remained unchanged over the entire pH interval, while a strong pH dependency was observed for the fluorescence emission of the mixture of carbon dots and free chlorine. The quenching capability of the free chlorine seems to be maximum at pH values around 4.0, which could be related to the equilibrium of the species of free chlorine in water, according to a previous publication [36]. In alkaline solutions, the predominant species is the hypochlorite ion but, at pH values below 7.5, hypochlorous acid becomes important. The chlorine species appears when the pH drops below 4.0. The oxidation ability of these species increases in the following order: ClO− < HClO < Cl2, which is in accordance with the behaviour observed in Fig. S6 [49]. Therefore, the selected pH was 4.0 in order to obtain a higher sensitivity for the analyte and to avoid eventual losses of gaseous Cl2.

The selectivity of the carbon dots was evaluated by measuring their fluorescence response to different compounds normally found in natural waters, in concentrations ranging from 10- to 100-fold its typical occurrence. As it can be seen from Fig. 4 , the tested compounds caused variations lower than 5% in the fluorescence signal of the carbon dots. Thus, the proposed method results appropriate for the determination of free chlorine either in tap, natural or swimming pool water samples.

The relationship between fluorescence signal (%RG) and free chlorine concentration was studied within the range of 20–500 μg L−1 of free chlorine. The straight line corresponding to the regression of %RG* vs. concentration (where %RG* is (%RG0–%RG)/%RG, %RG and %RG0 are the signals of carbon dots in presence and absence of free chlorine, respectively) showed a good linearity, and the linear equation was %RG* = 91.64 L μg−1 [free chlorine] – 2.81 with a determination coefficient of 0.9994 (six concentration levels, within the range of 20–500 μg L−1 of free chlorine). The limit of detection was of 6 μg L−1, whereas the limit of quantification was of 20 μg L−1, estimated as 3.3 and 10 times the blank standard deviation, respectively [50]. The precision, expressed as RSD%, was 4.6% (n = 6), and was evaluated for a free chlorine concentration of 150 μg L−1, under repeatability conditions.

The proposed method was applied for free chlorine determination in different water samples. The samples were analysed with both the standard method (4500-Cl G DPD method) and the proposed one using the portable device. It is important to note that, for comparison purposes, both methods were simultaneously carried out to avoid differences in the results arising from eventual chlorine species degradation. Table 1 summarizes the analysed samples and the concentrations of free chlorine found by both methods. In order to test the presence of bias, the values of free chlorine obtained by the proposed method were regressed against the ones obtained with the 4500-Cl G DPD method, using bivariate least squares [50]. The regression equation was y = (0.96 ± 0.02)x + (0.01 ± 0.01), and the correlation coefficient 0.997, indicating, in a qualitative manner, that both methods fit well to each other. Afterwards, the joint test for the slope and the intercept was applied to evaluate whether there were significant differences between the regression coefficients obtained and the theoretical values of zero intercept and unity slope [51,52]. Fig. 5 shows that the elliptical region of confidence centered on the obtained regression coefficients (i.e. 0.96 slope and 0.007 intercept) contains the theoretical point (1,0). Therefore, there are no significant statistical differences between both sets of results, considering an overall significance level of α = 0.05, which indicates that the proposed method is accurate and reliable.

The characteristics of the proposed method were compared with those of other optical methods found in the literature for the determination of free chlorine species in different water samples. Several colorimetric methods which use different devices, such as smartphones [13,14] and μPADs associated with scanners [11], were reported. The described procedures were simple and portable, but the detection limits obtained were higher in comparison with our method. Other colorimetric methods improved the detection limits, but they needed relatively long incubation times. In this sense, Xiong et al. reported a method that required 10 min to reach a stable signal [53], whereas Yu et al. proposed a photometric indirect free chlorine determination that took about 35 min [54]. Also, the standard method (4500-Cl G) consisted of a colorimetric determination reaching a detection limit of 0.02 mg L−1 [5].

On the other hand, several luminescent methods were found in the literature [46,55–60]. They used carbon dots [46,55,58], metallic quantum dots [56], metal-MOF nanoplates [57] as sensors, and carbon nitride nanoparticles [59]. In many of them, detection limits lower than 3 μg L−1 were reached [55–59], but the methods were designed to use a spectrofluorometer as an instrument, so they did not allow field measurements. Finally, carbon nitride nanoparticles were also used in a chemiluminescence determination of free chlorine with low detection limits [60].
In comparison with the methods compiled in Table 2 , our method reached low detection limits, even lower than the standard method [5], with the possibility of being used for on-site measures.

A new reliable and portable analytical method was developed for the determination of free chlorine in water samples. The procedure was based on the quenching generated by the free chlorine species on the native fluorescence of the carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and urea. The proposed method included a 3D printed device that made possible the in-situ measurements using a smartphone camera as detection system. The figures of merit indicated that the new method showed a satisfactory linear range, low detection limits and high selectivity. The results obtained for the analysis of different water samples were accurate, i.e. they showed good precision, and their comparison with those obtained with reference 4500-Cl G spectrophotometric method demonstrated the absence of bias. Another advantage is that the gadget is easy to handle for unskilled users. Therefore, the new procedure and the related handheld device could be suggested as a fast, inexpensive and trustworthy alternative for on-site determination of free chlorine.

D. Uriarte Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization, E. Vidal Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization, A. Canals Resources, review & editing, C. E. Domini Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, supervision, funding acquisition, M. Garrido Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, supervision, funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the financial support received from Universidad Nacional del Sur (PGI 24/Q099 and 24/Q086) and the ANPCyT (PICT-201-0659). The authors also thank Consejo Nacional de Investigaciones Científicas y Técnicas (CONICET) particularly for the doctoral grant of D. Uriarte.
